gene_description: Melatonin Receptor 1B — G-protein coupled receptor for melatonin in pancreatic beta cells. Variant rs10830963 increases receptor expression, enhancing melatonin-mediated inhibition of insulin secretion and raising fasting glucose.

source.title: Variants in MTNR1B influence fasting glucose levels
source.finding: MTNR1B rs10830963 G allele is strongly associated with increased fasting glucose (+0.07 mmol/L per allele, P=3.2×10⁻⁵⁰), decreased early-phase insulin response, and 9% increased risk of type 2 diabetes per allele (OR=1.09).

GG.phenotype: Elevated fasting glucose / impaired insulin secretion
GG.effect: Homozygous G carriers show significantly elevated fasting glucose (~0.14 mmol/L above baseline), reduced HOMA-B (beta-cell function), and impaired early-phase insulin secretion. Melatonin exerts stronger inhibition of insulin release. Eating late at night (when melatonin is high) worsens glucose tolerance in these individuals. Increased type 2 diabetes risk.
GG.recommendation: Avoid eating close to bedtime (finish meals 3+ hours before sleep). If using melatonin supplements, monitor blood glucose. Prefer early time-restricted feeding. Regular fasting glucose monitoring recommended. If diabetic, melatonin supplementation may impair glycemic control.

CG.effect: Heterozygous carriers show intermediate fasting glucose elevation (~0.07 mmol/L). Modest reduction in early-phase insulin secretion. Melatonin-glucose interaction is present but less pronounced.
CG.recommendation: Maintain regular meal timing and avoid late-night eating. Standard monitoring. Melatonin supplements unlikely to cause major issues but be aware of potential glucose effects.

GC.effect: Heterozygous carriers show intermediate fasting glucose elevation (~0.07 mmol/L). Modest reduction in early-phase insulin secretion. Melatonin-glucose interaction is present but less pronounced.
GC.recommendation: Maintain regular meal timing and avoid late-night eating. Standard monitoring. Melatonin supplements unlikely to cause major issues but be aware of potential glucose effects.

CC.effect: Wild-type with standard MTNR1B signaling. Normal fasting glucose regulation and insulin secretion from this locus. Melatonin does not excessively inhibit insulin release.
CC.recommendation: Standard dietary and metabolic guidelines. Melatonin supplements may be used without specific glucose concerns from this locus.
